sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Tam (AKA Glorious Leader) reports

There are old pilots and there are bold pilots, but there are no old bold pilots. So they say, and I’m sure it’s true. But I’ll give you a new flying aphorism: Don’t let your sage advice turn you into a naysayer.  

Stood on Dodd recently I and others I was speaking to talked ourselves out of flying for the day after what had been quite a feisty start to the day. Slinky wings going backwards on launch, vertical launches, definitely strong winds but frequently no lift on the ridge, and some bombed out. Strange conditions. But also definitely flyable and getting better as time progressed, as proved by the increasing numbers in the air. My fellow wise old men and I were however not convinced and talked ourselves out of even getting our wings out of bags even as some were getting away from the hill. In our defence, we weren’t the only ones who were suffering from ground-suck, some had left already and other groups were standing around sizing up the day. Though the decision had not yet been voiced, it was pretty clear we weren’t going to fly. Fast forward an hour or so, and fresh faces arrived at the hill keen to fly, and asking how it had been. We each took it in turn to “helpfully” share our wise old man knowledge and explain in sage terms why it was a bad idea to fly, despite the fact that it felt ok at the time and people were clearly flying. To our credit, I’m pretty sure we did manage to add in something like “it has to be your decision though, of course”. Driving away shortly afterwards I remembered some words from an article in Skywings many years ago - stay away from the naysayers. Yes, always ask those there what it has been like - you need factual information to make your own flying decisions - but don’t get dragged into negative spirals of thoughts that may put you off what may be for you a perfectly good flying opportunity. Talk politely to the naysayers, then move on! Talk to people who have flown if you can, to find out what it is it actually like in the air, you’ll get more useful information rather than ground based negative assessments.  There can be a fine line between giving good information and well meant but negative personal judgements.  If you’re giving advice, remember to try to be on the right side of that line - or “don’t let your sage advice turn you into a naysayer”.  I hope the recipients of my advice on Dodd that day ignored it and got a great flight!

We have recently made the decision to still hold an end of season club camping week-end in early September. There will be no Hike and Fly this year, and we have decided to have a rather less formalised camping experience, a camp out rather than fly-in perhaps. We will announce the date shortly (it will be either 4-6th or 11th-13th), and you won’t need tickets - just book with the farmer at the usual campsite (Lodge Park, Langcliffe). Basically an opportunity to have get together in a way that is not usually possible in our sport. Hopefully flyable days and coaching /task setting if the weather plays ball, and parabollox sessions around the campfire at night - it is impossible for you to have anything more important to do at that time of year!!

Fly safe everyone - the season is definitely here!

Tam

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

 A few changes to the coaching lineup this year. Above is the new map of where they’re all located.

Many thanks go to Trevor Birkbeck and Rhian Ebrey who’ve retired from flying and Kevin Gay, Chris Baird and Simon Goodman, who’ve moved location or onto different types of flight. Trevor in particular has a long history of assisting hang gliders, especially those going through to competition. See his write up in April’s Skywings. Thank you all for stepping up and helping other pilots improve and stay safe in the air.

As always, to get in touch with the coaches, including someone local to you, they’re all listed here:
https://dhpc.org.uk/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2977 

When getting your Pilot Rating;

We tend to go for exams in the Autumn as it leaves summer for going through all the Pilot Rating tasks that are required.

Not all tasks need to be seen in person.

https://www.bhpa.co.uk/documents/index. ... _Tasks.pdf

Tasks B1, C1, C2, C3, C7 will need to be seen in person and the rest can be done by showing a coach your tracklogs / flight record book / video. C6 to D2 get signed off having met a coach who’s seen your flying and talked to you about glider recovery.

When you meet up with a coach, have your pilot task book with you so that any tasks that do get completed can be signed off there and then. Also, don't forget some form of proof that you're a BHPA member - that can either be your membership card or the online membership proof.

sections.title: Spelling Test - Stags Fell
sections.content:
Martin Baxter muses

Released from the responsibilities of Chairman, I now have the time to reflect on unimportant trivia, or parabollocks as it’s called in the trade.  My wife tells me that I should be doing something more important, but when you’re ‘on the spectrum’ sometimes you just can’t help yourself…

So the burning question; is it Stags Fell or Staggs Fell?  Let’s forget the apostrophe and whether (or is that Wether?) the fell belongs to a single stag or a herd, otherwise this could be a long newsletter.

I have conducted a comprehensive survey (below) and as you can see the vote suggests one ‘g’ not 2.  With apologies to the late great DJ Steve Wright: ‘no (extra) g’.

 

OS 1:25K map.  The only Dales location depicted as a Hang Gliding Site.

But we’re not the only ones to get confused:

Link to the history of the Quarries at Stags (and Stag’s) Fell, with mention of the DHPC, here.

Next month - What’s in a name: Hawkswick, Windbank or Knipe Scar; and why it could matter.

sections.title: Saturday 25th April
sections.content:
Pete Logan reports

Convergence is hard stuff to work when there’s no cloud around….



A big convergence line was threatening to be somewhere over the Dales but with the frustration of little wind to stick the thermals to hillsides. Forty or more of us chose Hawkswick and were ready to be off at 11ish as some lift was coming through. There seemed to be inversions at 850m then again at 1200m so it was bouncy going with lift being strong in places and then quite dribbly. Looking further afield a line of (maybe haar) cloud was just viewable through the murk lying over the North Yorks Moors which was indicating the NE breeze coming in.

Inversions and some strong spring air led to me having a bit extra workload as I'd switched to a lightweight harness for the day. It's a bit of a lesson in the difference a harness can make to your wing and the flying feel.

Ten or so of us left in a couple of gaggles with the first going back onto the Buckden ridge and finding little success. It had become becalmed on launch by this point. The rest of us grouped up at 1200m and made our way north to start our outward leg of a flight to Kirby Stephen and back. A long glide over Langstrothdale Chase was rewarded by multiple climb outs over Strans Gill and up onto Yockenthwaite Moor.

Then onto Raydale and Semer where a lone glider ahead of us was slowly climbing back out. I'd got low and dropped back by this point and took time to find a 3 up to bring me back to 1500m or so. Jake saw this (his first XC back after his op) and joined under me but I pressed on over the Semer ridge towards cumulus..... and a glide to the deck with all the others in front of me.

Regroup at the Crown and some lifts back ensued (thanks Rosie, Martin). Jake probably had the best of the day with a 50km out and return. The two Jacobs managed a later flight that crossed Wensleydale, but not my much. The convergence was very squirrelly that day

I’m not much of one for flight pictures, but here’s one of the gig that Chris and I were at the night before. It’s nice to get some fresh air to recover from a bit of a night out.

Although some nice shots here from Kacper… https://www.facebook.com/xcjunkies.org/posts/pfbid02LpD878Be5GppiHUAdzqLsyGtyyUUWVVHFK3KWDBYLk9rDWRWsddbqtUgYXqM4B1d

sections.title: British Paragliding Cup (BPC)
sections.content:
Ed Cleasby reports

The BPCup arrives 4th June until 7th and is based at Dalesbridge. I will be the local liaison and as part of that will post early each day what site is likely to be chosen. Probably on the ‘flying plans’. This year was well over subscribed so given good flying weather it could be busy.

sections.title: Radio Gaga
sections.content:
Pete Logan reports

The club is thinking of getting back into providing some EC - electronic conspicuity again. Uptake and is good amongst all of our instruments (FANET+, FLARM, ADS-L, etc.) but there are blind spots as we float over the hills in and out of phone signal. We had started to go down this path before with AirWhere which almost got off the ground but now these technologies are a bit easier. We’re looking for a willing volunteer to be the radio person for the club. It would involve:
- Determining if the club needs ground stations in the first place.

- Finding out which tech would be suitable (Soft RF, PilotAware etc.)

- Finding some locations for stations and installing them.

If that sounds like a project you’d be up for then contact Pete at coaching@dhpc.org.uk. I’ve got about half of what’s needed in my head so you won’t be alone, plus a cupboard full of potentially useful kit I can pass on. Skills wise, if you’ve heard of a Raspberry Pi, that’s a start.

The club’s interest in this is the safety of our members. Someone out on an XC or hike and fly who’s down in a valley with no phone reception would appreciate emergency services knowing where they are and being easily findable using sites like puretrack.io. The Cumbria club just put some of these ground stations in for exactly those reasons.

sections.title: Safety: if it doesn't feel right
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann reports

As many readers of this magazine might be aware, I've had my share of big (50-80+%) collapses (and one full frontal). It certainly got to the point where it felt like I had one ‘event’ nearly every flight when the air was a little active. With 200hrs on this wing I am dealing with them ok (practice makes perfect after all…yeah right), but it just isn't normal. Is it the wing, is it me, or me on this wing?

Of note is that on my first (low-B) wing, I had not exactly the same big events but very regularly did have small(ish) tip collapses. The main thing I hated about it was that I had soo little feedback from the wing they were hard to notice before they happened.

Typically my current wing felt ‘skittisch’; it would turn sharpish with not much brake. I could typically thermal with only a little inner brake, pull more and it'd turn too tight or fall back. In active air and active flying it felt a bit like the high-spirited half-arab pony my dad got me as a kid; always ready for an unexpected jolt and never out of energy. Like this wing, I loved that pony but she was too much of a handful.

I wondered if my mediocre piloting might have been aggrevated during SIV late 2024 on my current wing, when a ‘spin appreciation’ drill went wrong, ending up having to throw my reserve in earnest. Then off course there was my crash a year later, which made me nervous for a while. However, I was pretty sure I was over that. But was I??

I was flying the 75-95kg wing, whilst well within ‘spec’, on the light side. Even at post-winter weight myself, in full winter gear and with my new Arrow harness (0.7 kg heavier than my previous Delight3) I was only around 88kg AUW. During summer I'll have been several kg below that.

Towards the end of last season I started using 2kg of ballast in the form of 2 liter water; easy to drink&dump on retrieve but enough extra weight to carry uphill to not want more.

Being at 88-90 kg AUW felt better with less skittishness, and became my default. The few times I forgot my ballast bottle, the behaviour seemed to revert although it's always hard to tell from a single specific day.

With the onset of spring and some decidedly rowdy air, I took a several more collapses. I got away with them fine, but it was time to get to the bottom of this. After a mere 200 hours of “dealing with it”, this thing was now seriously holding me back and, to be honest, felt like an accident waiting to happen.

So I contacted our Chief Coach Pete and proposed possible causes:

A. Bad pilot

- I'm doing something fundamentally wrong whilst thermalling.

- I'm on the light side (~90kg with 2kg ballast), without the ballast the wing is notably more jittery/fluttering.

B. Bad wing

- During my SIV 2 years ago I ended up in the Lake. It was serviced and trimmed shortly after by the Loft and, another 80hr on, again end 2025 (after my IB crash but was already booked). Neither found an issue.

-. Wing tip internal load too low (due to v poor porosity, although I checked this specifically last year at the FlyIn with Ginger's official meter and was ok)

C. ???

We went back and forth (on the side discussing the benefit of a 360° cameras for coaching). However, the quickest and easiest thing to try was to add a few more kg ballast.

So the next flying day I trotted up the hill with my ballast bottle as well as two 1kg ‘soft’ diving weights, bringing my actual on-the-scales AUW to 92 kg.

And WOW… I had a new wing!  Solid, not a flutter. Turning takes a bit more input as expected, but I can thermal easily and (finally!) with inner brake at the mallion and the outer hand hanging out and feeling the turn. After 250(!) hours flying, this suddenly just happened automatically.

I've now been flying at 92kg for 9 hours over 6 flying days this month, and the wing has stayed rock solid in even rather boisterous air.

I traded the water and lead for a dry bag, tiny trowel and luggage scales. I stand on the scales packed to leave for the day and know exactly how much (wet) dirt to dig on take-off to get to 92kg AUW.

So what changed?  Easy to say the root cause was the wing “needs” high AUW. But I'm not sure that was the whole story. It is possible that the extra ‘stiffness’ in the wing subconsciously gave me the confidence to pack on more brake when flying active and thermalling.

The next test will be to take some weight off again and see what happens…

Moral of the story: if you are struggling with an aspect of your flying, don't struggle with it on your own but get in touch with a (senior / chief) coach, discuss it and work out an action plan. You'll be a better and safer pilot at the end of it.

sections.title: BRITISH OPEN SERIES (BOS) 9th May to 16th May
sections.content:
Andrew Woods reports

The first round of the Hang Gliding British open series will be taking place from Saturday May 9th to Saturday May 16th. There will be around 25 pilots taking part, in 3 categories. Class 1, Class 1 Sport and Class 5 (Rigids).

As organiser I would just like to share some of the background work that has been done to ensure the smooth running of the competition.

After seeking the permission from the committee to use the DHPC sites Jon Sacre and myself have visited the site TO/BLF owners or land managers to make them aware that there will be a HG competition in the area and that we may be using the club sites, subject to the weather.

All farmers visited were extremely receptive to us and the competition. We exchanged contact details. We informed them that if we were likely to use the site on a particular day then we would give them a call, out of courtesy the evening before.

One of the issues with HG in the Dales is lack of accessible hills for a southerly wind direction. With our Kit weighing in at around 45kg it makes some of the walk up near impossible or we have to do it in 2 trips. I approached northern sites officer Tom about the possibility of using Stags Fell. He felt with the great relationship they have with the estate management office that it could possibly happen. The DHPC committee were approached and with their agreement, Tom messaged the estate office. They were extremely positive in a temporary lifting of the members only rule. They have also allowed us to take vehicles up the track. This will only be allowed for 2 or 3 vehicles to drop the gliders off. They will then return to the main road to park.

The HG Comps Panel are extremely thankful for the work that has gone in the background to allow this. There will be several DHPC members that are competing and will ensure the site rules are adhered to at all times. A site briefing will be given at the morning briefing and again on the hill. This is just not for Stags Fell but for all sites that we may fly.

The sites we are likely to use are:

Wether Fell

Tailbridge

Stags Fell

Dodd Fell (east)

Semerwater SE and NNW

Fremington

Nappa Scar

We may also be using The Model Ridge on the North Yorkshire Moors.

Club Free flyers PG/HG are still welcome to fly the same site but just be mindful when the launch window opens (usually around (1230) the ridge could get busy with unfamiliar aircraft. Given good conditions we will soon be out of the way!
